## Thesis Organization and Chapter Overview

This thesis is organized to move from problem formulation and literature
motivation to agent design, implementation, and empirical evaluation in a
microstructure-aware trading setting.

Chapter 1 introduces the problem context, defines the research scope, and
presents the methodological framework used throughout the thesis.

Chapter 2 reviews the literature most directly relevant to the present research
question. It focuses on market microstructure evidence motivating
order-book-derived features, the structural case for reinforcement learning in
trading, and prior RL trading applications with attention to action-space
design, transaction-cost modeling, and evaluation rigor.

Chapter 3 places reinforcement learning within the broader landscape of machine
learning and algorithmic trading system design. It contrasts supervised,
unsupervised, and reinforcement learning approaches, then situates the trading
agent within a larger trading-system architecture and alternative decision
paradigms.

Chapter 4 develops the reinforcement learning foundations required for the
thesis, with particular emphasis on the progression from earlier methods to TD3
and on the distinctions that matter for continuous-control trading problems.

Chapter 5 presents the design of the trading agent itself. It defines the
continuous action space, the microstructure-aware state representation, the
reward formulation, and the main policy and value-function design choices used
in the empirical system.

Chapter 6 describes the implementation of the research pipeline, including data
preparation, configuration-driven feature engineering, code structure, and the
TD3-based training workflow used to produce reproducible experiments.

Chapter 7 reports the empirical results. It combines experiment snapshots,
performance evaluation, statistical validation, and robustness assessment to
separate promising outcomes from noise, overfitting, or fragile conclusions.

Chapter 8 synthesizes the findings, discusses the main limitations of the
current framework, and draws implications for future research and practical
trading-system development.
